content:
A Look at Amazon’s AI Hiring Tool and the Ethics Behind It

Over the last year or so, I’ve spent a lot more time (then I’d like to admit) updating my resume, writing cover letters, and applying for jobs. Like many other job seekers, I’ve found myself wondering if a real person ever looked at my application or if it was filtered out before anyone had the chance. As companies continue to use artificial intelligence (AI) to help sort through hundreds or even thousands of applications, that question becomes more relevant than ever. AI has become a valuable tool for employers because it can save time by quickly scanning resumes and identifying candidates who appear to be the best fit. However, when AI starts making decisions that affect people’s careers, it’s important to ask whether those decisions are being made fairly. Can an algorithm be unbiased, or does it simply learn from the people who created it and the data it was given?

One of the most well-known examples of this issue is Amazon’s experimental AI hiring tool. Although it was designed to make recruiting more efficient, the system ended up creating ethical concerns about fairness, bias, and accountability. In this blog, I’ll take a closer look at what happened, why it happened, and what it teaches us about using AI in hiring.

So… Where Did Things Go Wrong?

This isn’t just a story about Amazon. It’s a reminder that data analysts play an important role in how AI systems are built. The data used to train an AI model can directly influence the decisions it makes, which is why ethics should be considered from the very beginning of the development process.

So back in 2014, Amazon secretly started developing an AI recruiting tool. It was designed to look at 10 years of hiring data and rate incoming resumes on a scale of one to five based on patterns from previous hiring decisions. The problem was that, for most of those 10 years, the tech industry was heavily male-dominated, so most of the people being hired were men. Over time, the AI learned that pattern and developed a gender bias. It even started discarding resumes that included words like “women’s,” such as “women’s chess club.” Once Amazon realized what was happening, they scrapped the project in 2018. Since then, the company has taken a more careful approach by using newer AI tools that go through much more testing before being used to help reduce bias in the hiring process.

When History Repeats Itself…

One of the biggest ethical concerns with Amazon’s hiring tool was fairness. AI is supposed to help make decisions based on skills and qualifications, not someone’s gender. Unfortunately, because the system was trained using hiring data from a male-dominated industry, it started associating male applicants with being more successful. As a result, qualified women were being ranked lower simply because of patterns the AI had learned from the past.

Behind the Curtain

Ooohh transparency, what a funny thought when it comes to a company secretly screening your resume without your knowledge. Which brings me to my other ethical concern. Most people applying for a job probably assume a recruiter or hiring manager is looking at their resume. In Amazon’s case, many applicants had no idea an AI system was helping evaluate them, and they certainly didn’t know how it was making those decisions. If someone was rejected because the AI ranked their resume lower, there was no explanation or opportunity to understand why. Without that transparency, it’s hard for people to trust the hiring process.

Transparency asks questions like:

Did applicants know AI was screening them?

Did they know how the AI ranked resumes?

If they were rejected, could they find out why?

Could they challenge the AI’s decision?

The answer to most of those questions was no. Transparency doesn’t mean a company has to reveal every detail of its AI model. It does mean applicants should understand when AI is being used and have some idea of how important decisions about their applications are being made.

Own Your Mistakes

Another ethical issue I noticed was accountability. When Amazon discovered its AI hiring tool was unfairly ranking candidates, the company shut the project down. While that was an important step, I couldn’t find a public apology to the applicants who may have been affected or much explanation about what went wrong. It made me wonder who should be held responsible when AI makes decisions that impact people’s lives.

For what it’s worth, in my opinion; responsibility shouldn’t fall on the AI itself. AI can only work with the data and instructions given. It’s up to the company and the people developing the system to make sure it’s tested properly, monitored for bias, and corrected when problems are found. That’s what accountability looks like in data science.

Will AI Take Our Jobs?

So in all fairness, mistakes were made and corrected by Amazon. But this just teaches us how important it is for AI systems to be monitored carefully for correction; AI is a tool and not a final decision maker.

Think about it like this:

AI can process 100,000 resumes much faster than a person.

AI can spot patterns humans might miss.

AI can save companies a huge amount of time.

Those are all positives.

But…

AI doesn’t understand fairness.

AI doesn’t understand the context.

AI doesn’t stop and think; it simply follows the patterns it learned.

And THAT is where humans come in.

Final Thoughts

The biggest lesson I took away from this case is that AI shouldn’t be making important decisions on its own. It can be an incredible tool for sorting through large amounts of data, but there should always be people checking the results. If Amazon had been monitoring the system more closely, they may have caught the gender bias much earlier. AI can help speed up the hiring process, but it shouldn’t replace human judgment when someone’s career is on the line.
description: Did a human ever see my resume
title: Your resume never reached a human

content:
When “Anonymous” Isn’t Enough

Imagine; it’s the end of the semester and you have really struggled with the course, you feel the teacher has favored certain students, and the coursework didn’t make sense. You log in one day and there is a survey for the class that asks how the course was delivered and did it meet your expectations. You think to yourself, “now is my chance to express my concerns with this course! It says it stays anonymous…….right?” And now you begin to worry, because it says it’s anonymous, but how do I know they’re not lying?

Most students (myself included) have completed an online course survey at some point. These surveys typically ask whether the course met expectations, whether the instructor was effective, and how the course could improve. Many of them reassure participants that their responses are anonymous. But what happens when students must log in using their username and password before completing the survey?

At first glance, this may seem contradictory. If a system requires authentication, can it really be anonymous? This question highlights one of the ethical challenges in data science: maintaining trust while collecting meaningful data.

In this blog, I’ll break down some of the ethical concerns in this survey and talk about why privacy, transparency, and trust matter just as much as the data itself.

Understanding the Scenario

Students receive a survey after completing a course. Before accessing it, they must log in using their school username and password. The survey then says that the responses will remain anonymous.

Executive management plans to use the survey results to figure out which courses require redesign or improvement. As the assigned Data Analyst, my responsibility is to evaluate whether the survey design raises ethical concerns and recommend the improvements.

Although the purpose of improving educational programs is beneficial, the way data is collected can significantly affect both the students trust and the accuracy of the data collected.

Ethical Concern #1: Authentication vs. Anonymity

The first ethical concern is the contradiction between requiring login credentials while promising anonymity. From a technical perspective, both statements could be true, but how would the students know? The login process may simply verify that the respondent is enrolled in the course before generating a random survey identifier that removes all personal information……however, the survey never explains how this process works.

Without transparency, Students may assume their responses can be traced back to them and in that case never respond truthfully. Whether this assumption is true, the thought may discourage honest feedback.

In the end, Ethics in data science extends beyond technical implementation. Building trust with participants is equally important.

Ethical Concern #2: Response Bias

When students believe their identities may be known, they will likely alter their responses.

For example, a student who disliked a course may choose a higher rating out of concern that negative comments could affect their relationship with an instructor, which may lead to bad marks. This introduces response bias. At the end of the day, if people don’t trust the survey enough to answer honestly, the data loses value. Management could end up making decisions based on feedback that doesn’t really reflect how students feel.

From a data analyst’s perspective, protecting participant confidence directly improves data quality.

Ethical Concern #3: Lack of Transparency

Another ethical concern involves informed consent.

The survey explains that responses are anonymous but does not answer several important questions:

Who can access the data?

How long will responses be stored?

Will login information be deleted?

Can administrators reconnect responses to students?

Is participation voluntary?

Without this information, students cannot make a fully informed decision about sharing their opinions. Transparency is a fundamental principle of responsible data collection.

Ethical Concern #4: The Survey Question Itself

The survey begins with the statement:

“This course met all of the course objectives.”

Participants rate this statement on a scale from 0 to 10. The problem here is that some students may have entered the course with 0 expectations, so how do they know if the course objectives were met? Students may not fully understand the official course objectives. Different students may interpret the statement differently, making responses inconsistent.

I also think one question only tells part of the story. A student’s experience is made up of a lot of different factors, and trying to sum it all up with one rating can leave out important feedback. Allowing students to ask questions exploring instructor effectiveness, course materials, workload, and learning outcomes would likely produce more meaningful insights.

Recommendations

As the data analyst, I would recommend several improvements before distributing the survey.

First, explain why login credentials are needed and how anonymity is protected. Even a short statement explaining that usernames are separated from survey responses after authentication would help build trust.

Second, include a privacy notice outlining:

who has access to the results,

how the data will be stored,

how long it will be retained,

and whether participation is voluntary.

Third, redesign the survey to include multiple questions measuring different aspects of the course rather than relying primarily on one overall rating.

Finally, I’d only report the survey results instead of showing individual responses. Even if names aren’t included, it can sometimes be easy to guess who is in a small class.

What If Management Refuses?

Sometimes the higher ups choose not to implement ethical recommendations. If management rejected my suggestions while insisting that the survey posed no ethical concerns, I would still have professional responsibilities. As a data analyst, I would document my recommendations, explain the potential privacy and data quality risks, and ensure my concerns were communicated through proper channels. Even if nothing changes right away, I think it’s still important to document those concerns. It shows the risks were identified and gives the organization a chance to revisit them if issues come up later. Ethical professionals cannot always control final decisions, but they are responsible for providing informed, evidence-based advice.

Final Thoughts

This blog shows that ethical data collection involves more than simply protecting personal information. It also requires transparency, participant trust, and thoughtful survey design.

At the end of the day, it comes down to trust. Even if the survey is anonymous, students are less likely to be honest if they don’t understand how their identity is being protected.
title: The Privacy Paradox: Logging In to an AnonymousSurvey

content:
Mathjax

Math equations can be rendered using Mathjax syntax with AMS symbol support.

Optionally enable this on a per-page basis by adding mathjax: true to your frontmatter.

Then, use $$ ... $$ on a line by itself to render a block equation:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

The raw version is:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

Write in-line equations with \\( ... \\) , like \( x^n / y \) . It’s easy!

Write in-line equations with `\\( ... \\)` , like \\( x^n / y \\) . It's easy!
title: Render Math With Mathjax

content:
Emoji can be enabled in a Hugo project in a number of ways. :zap:

Emoji Support

The emojify function can be called directly in templates or Inline Shortcodes.

To enable emoji globally, set enableEmoji to true in your site’s configuration and then you can type emoji shorthand codes directly in content files; e.g.

The Emoji cheat sheet is a useful reference for emoji shorthand codes.

N.B. The above steps enable Unicode Standard emoji characters and sequences in Hugo, however the rendering of these glyphs depends on the browser and the platform. To style the emoji you can either use a third party emoji font or a font stack; e.g.

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}
title: Emoji Support

content:
Hugo ships with several Embedded Shortcodes for rich content, along with a Privacy Config and a set of Simple Shortcodes that enable static and no-JS versions of various social media embeds.

X Simple Shortcode

{{< x user="GoHugoIO" id="1315233626070503424" >}}

Vimeo Simple Shortcode

{{< vimeo 146022717 >}}


Youtube Simple Shortcode

{{< youtube w7Ft2ymGmfc >}}


Theme Custom Shortcodes

These shortcodes are not Hugo built-ins, but are provided by the theme.

Responsive Images with Cloudinary

You can learn more about this here.

Set the cloudinary_cloud_name parameter in your site config to use this shortcode.

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" >}}

Note that you do not include the file extension (e.g. .png) in the src parameter, as the shortcode will automatically determine the best quality and format for the user’s device.

Optionally, you can customize the general CSS styles for the image:

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" style="max-width:60%" >}}
title: Rich Content
